claude::e7c36d02a5528ecf358e307b68f67df1.what_evidence: the model represents an organization's defenses as successive slices of cheese, each with holes (latent and active failures), and that a harm event occurs only when the holes momentarily align... Reason's distinction between active failures (unsafe acts at the sharp end — e.g., grabbing the wrong vial) and latent conditions (organizational decisions whose consequences lie dormant — understaffing, ambiguous policies, poor lighting)
claude::e7c36d02a5528ecf358e307b68f67df1.what_explanation: The student provides a clear and specific description of the Swiss Cheese Model, including its core mechanism, key terminology, and concrete examples of both active failures and latent conditions, fully meeting the rubric criteria for a score of 2.
claude::e7c36d02a5528ecf358e307b68f67df1.why_evidence: I had been instinctively treating individual errors as individual problems, especially in stories about medication errors that named the person involved. The Swiss Cheese Model reframes the same event as a system that allowed the error to reach the patient — and reframes the goal of safety work as adding or repairing defensive layers, not punishing individuals at the sharp end.
claude::e7c36d02a5528ecf358e307b68f67df1.why_explanation: The student clearly articulates why the learning mattered by describing a specific prior mental model that was changed, explaining the personal insight gained, and connecting it to a broader implication for safety practice, fully satisfying the rubric criteria for a score of 2.
claude::e7c36d02a5528ecf358e307b68f67df1.how_evidence: (1) committing, when I encounter a near miss, to ask "what other defenses should have caught this?" before asking "who made the mistake?"; (2) asking my preceptor to share one institutional RCA with me and explicitly map the Swiss Cheese layers and their holes; and (3) carrying the model into my own dispensing practice — I will think of my verification step as one slice of cheese among many, not the last line of defense
claude::e7c36d02a5528ecf358e307b68f67df1.how_explanation: The student outlines three distinct, specific, and actionable steps for applying the learning during rotations, each tied directly to the concepts learned, fully meeting the rubric criteria for a score of 2.

llama-3.3-70b::e7c36d02a5528ecf358e307b6.what_evidence: Today's class introduced James Reason's Swiss Cheese Model as the foundational systems-thinking framework for patient safety. I learned that the model represents an organization's defenses as successive slices of cheese, each with holes (latent and active failures), and that a harm event occurs only when the holes momentarily align.
llama-3.3-70b::e7c36d02a5528ecf358e307b6.what_explanation: The student provides a clear and specific description of the key learnings from the class, including the Swiss Cheese Model and its components, which aligns with a score of 2 on the WHAT dimension of the rubric. This description demonstrates a strong understanding of the material covered in the class.
llama-3.3-70b::e7c36d02a5528ecf358e307b6.why_evidence: This shifted my mental model of safety in an important way. I had been instinctively treating individual errors as individual problems, especially in stories about medication errors that named the person involved. The Swiss Cheese Model reframes the same event as a system that allowed the error to reach the patient — and reframes the goal of safety work as adding or repairing defensive layers, not punishing individuals at the sharp end.
llama-3.3-70b::e7c36d02a5528ecf358e307b6.why_explanation: The student clearly explains why the learning mattered, including how it changed their mental model of safety and how it reframes the goal of safety work, which aligns with a score of 2 on the WHY dimension of the rubric. This analysis demonstrates personal insight and a deeper understanding of the material.
llama-3.3-70b::e7c36d02a5528ecf358e307b6.how_evidence: I will apply this in my rotations by (1) committing, when I encounter a near miss, to ask 'what other defenses should have caught this?' before asking 'who made the mistake?'; (2) asking my preceptor to share one institutional RCA with me and explicitly map the Swiss Cheese layers and their holes; and (3) carrying the model into my own dispensing practice — I will think of my verification step as one slice of cheese among many, not the last line of defense, so I am realistic about the system around me.
llama-3.3-70b::e7c36d02a5528ecf358e307b6.how_explanation: The student provides a clear, specific, and actionable future plan for applying the learning, including concrete steps they will take in their rotations and practice, which aligns with a score of 2 on the HOW dimension of the rubric. This plan demonstrates a strong commitment to integrating the learning into their future work.

llama-3.3-70b::8d626f11e584c7460bc965a1d.what_evidence: This session covered 'speak-up culture' and TeamSTEPPS as concrete tools for addressing one of the most stubborn problems in patient safety — the failure of team members to voice safety concerns to a more senior or more confident colleague. I learned the TeamSTEPPS techniques specifically designed to lower the cost of speaking up: CUS ('I'm Concerned, I'm Uncomfortable, this is a Safety issue'), the two-challenge rule (if your concern is dismissed once, you must voice it a second time before deferring), and DESC scripts for delivering structured feedback after a critical event.
llama-3.3-70b::8d626f11e584c7460bc965a1d.what_explanation: The student provides a clear and specific description of key learnings, activities, and concepts, including the session's coverage of 'speak-up culture' and TeamSTEPPS, as well as the specific techniques learned, meeting the criteria for a score of 2 on the WHAT dimension.

CUS and the two-challenge rule give me both permission and language.
llama-3.3-70b::8d626f11e584c7460bc965a1d.why_explanation: The student clearly explains why the learning mattered, including personal insight and supporting evidence, such as their previous worries about 'how to push back' and how the learned techniques provide 'permission and language', meeting the criteria for a score of 2 on the WHY dimension.

claude::712167a3a2eb6970372eecd3059fa95c.what_evidence: the evidence-based components of effective disclosure are (1) timeliness (within 24–48 hours of identification), (2) involvement of the clinician most directly involved alongside an institutional representative, (3) clear factual description without speculation, (4) explicit apology, and (5) follow-up communication as the investigation progresses
claude::712167a3a2eb6970372eecd3059fa95c.what_explanation: The student provides a clear and specific enumeration of the five evidence-based components of disclosure and references concrete data (University of Michigan program outcomes), fully meeting the rubric's standard for a Score 2.
claude::712167a3a2eb6970372eecd3059fa95c.why_evidence: What this learning shifted for me was the assumption that disclosure and legal self-protection are inevitably in tension. The data suggest the opposite... I also realized that disclosure is a skill that requires practice — saying 'I'm sorry' at the right moment in the right way is not innate, and clinicians who are not trained tend to under-disclose, speculate, or apologize defensively.
claude::712167a3a2eb6970372eecd3059fa95c.why_explanation: The student clearly explains why the learning mattered by articulating a shifted personal assumption, supporting it with referenced evidence, and adding personal insight about disclosure as a learnable skill, fully satisfying the Score 2 criteria.
claude::712167a3a2eb6970372eecd3059fa95c.how_evidence: (1) asking my preceptor if the institution has a disclosure protocol and whether I can review one redacted disclosure summary to study the language and structure; (2) practicing a brief disclosure script with my preceptor in a low-stakes simulated scenario before I am ever in a real one; and (3) committing, if I am ever directly involved in an error that reaches a patient during my career, to participate in the disclosure rather than allow the institution to handle it alone
claude::712167a3a2eb6970372eecd3059fa95c.how_explanation: The student outlines three specific, actionable steps — reviewing a protocol, practicing a script with a preceptor, and making a personal professional commitment — which clearly meets the Score 2 standard for a concrete and actionable future plan.

llama-3.3-70b::712167a3a2eb6970372eecd30.what_evidence: Today's class focused on disclosure and apology after a harmful medical error — the practice of telling patients and families honestly what happened, why it happened, what the institution will do about it, and expressing genuine regret. I learned that the evidence-based components of effective disclosure are (1) timeliness (within 24–48 hours of identification), (2) involvement of the clinician most directly involved alongside an institutional representative, (3) clear factual description without speculation, (4) explicit apology, and (5) follow-up communication as the investigation progresses.
llama-3.3-70b::712167a3a2eb6970372eecd30.what_explanation: The student provides a clear and specific description of key learnings, activities, or concepts, meeting the criteria for a score of 2, as they outline the components of effective disclosure and the topic of the class. This description is detailed and directly related to the subject matter.
llama-3.3-70b::712167a3a2eb6970372eecd30.why_evidence: What this learning shifted for me was the assumption that disclosure and legal self-protection are inevitably in tension. The data suggest the opposite. The ethical case for disclosure is straightforward; what surprised me was that the institutional case is also strong.
llama-3.3-70b::712167a3a2eb6970372eecd30.why_explanation: The student clearly explains why the learning mattered, including personal insight and supporting evidence from the data, which warrants a score of 2, as they reflect on how their assumptions were challenged and provide reasoning for the importance of disclosure.
llama-3.3-70b::712167a3a2eb6970372eecd30.how_evidence: I will apply this by (1) asking my preceptor if the institution has a disclosure protocol and whether I can review one redacted disclosure summary to study the language and structure; (2) practicing a brief disclosure script with my preceptor in a low-stakes simulated scenario before I am ever in a real one; and (3) committing, if I am ever directly involved in an error that reaches a patient during my career, to participate in the disclosure rather than allow the institution to handle it alone — that is part of what I owe the patient.
llama-3.3-70b::712167a3a2eb6970372eecd30.how_explanation: The student outlines a clear, specific, and actionable future plan for applying the learning, which aligns with the criteria for a score of 2, as they detail concrete steps to take in order to integrate the knowledge into their practice.

claude::965693c187c4b3035055b7fd174c9505.what_evidence: the report estimated 44,000–98,000 preventable deaths per year in U.S. hospitals from medical errors and reframed safety as an emergent property of healthcare systems rather than the result of individual diligence. We reviewed the report's main recommendations — establish a national focus on safety, identify and learn from errors through reporting systems, raise standards through oversight bodies, and implement safety systems at the point of care — and tracked which have made progress (e.g., CMS-mandated reporting, accreditation standards) and which remain unfinished (e.g., interoperable error reporting, consistent disclosure practices).
claude::965693c187c4b3035055b7fd174c9505.what_explanation: The student provides specific statistics, named frameworks, and concrete examples of both completed and unfinished recommendations, meeting the rubric's standard for a clear and specific description of key learnings and concepts.
claude::965693c187c4b3035055b7fd174c9505.why_evidence: Without the IOM report's premise — that errors are produced by systems, not bad apples — none of the specific tools we have studied (RCA, Just Culture, Swiss Cheese, FMEA) make sense. It also clarified for me why patient safety is sometimes politically contested even within healthcare: 'systems thinking' is sometimes resisted as deflecting responsibility from individuals, and being aware of that history will help me communicate about safety work more thoughtfully on rotations.
claude::965693c187c4b3035055b7fd174c9505.why_explanation: The student goes beyond stating general importance by explaining how the IOM framework underpins other course tools and offering personal insight into the political tensions around systems thinking, supported with specific examples — fully meeting the rubric's criteria for Score 2.
claude::965693c187c4b3035055b7fd174c9505.how_evidence: I will apply this by (1) reading the original IOM report in full before my first inpatient rotation; (2) committing, when I encounter resistance to systems-based safety thinking on rotation, to explain the underlying premise calmly rather than assume bad faith; and (3) using 'To Err Is Human' as my internal frame whenever I am tempted to attribute a near miss to a single individual — first asking what about the system made the error possible.
claude::965693c187c4b3035055b7fd174c9505.how_explanation: The student outlines three distinct, specific, and actionable steps with clear triggers and contexts for application, fully satisfying the rubric's requirement for a clear, specific, and actionable future plan.

I learned that the report estimated 44,000–98,000 preventable deaths per year in U.S. hospitals from medical errors and reframed safety as an emergent property of healthcare systems rather than the result of individual diligence.
llama-3.3-70b::965693c187c4b3035055b7fd1.what_explanation: The student provides a clear and specific description of key learnings, including the report's main recommendations and its impact on the field, which aligns with a score of 2 according to the rubric.

llama-3.3-70b::965693c187c4b3035055b7fd1.why_explanation: The student clearly explains why the learning mattered, including personal insight into how the learning reframed their understanding of patient safety and its tools, which supports a score of 2.

claude::6b5f6331c4a97ac68fc31c08030ebedc.what_evidence: a sentinel event is a patient safety event resulting in death, permanent harm, or severe temporary harm — and that TJC requires accredited organizations to conduct a formal RCA within 45 days, with a corrective action plan that includes accountable owners and outcome measures. We reviewed the most-reported sentinel events: wrong-site surgery, retained foreign objects, medication errors causing harm, falls with serious injury, and patient suicide.
claude::6b5f6331c4a97ac68fc31c08030ebedc.what_explanation: The student provides specific, detailed descriptions of key concepts including the definition of sentinel events, the 45-day RCA requirement, corrective action plan components, and a concrete list of the most-reported sentinel event types, fully meeting the rubric's standard for a score of 2.
claude::6b5f6331c4a97ac68fc31c08030ebedc.why_evidence: What this clarified for me was the difference between an internal safety review and a regulatory-mandated RCA. The personal piece was understanding that, when I am eventually involved in a sentinel event, my role is not to wait for the report — it is to participate substantively in the RCA so that the lessons live in clinical practice, not just in the corrective action document. I had been imagining sentinel events as something that happens to an institution; the class reframed them as something an institution learns from when its people show up to the analysis.

claude::65feb150339293c4489e88821adb3d8d.what_evidence: a workaround is a routine bypass of a designed safety control — for example, scanning a printed copy of a wristband instead of the patient, or overriding an automated dispensing cabinet alert as a matter of habit. Diane Vaughan's term 'normalization of deviance' describes the gradual social process by which a workaround becomes accepted: the workaround produces no harm in any single instance, the team becomes desensitized to it, and within months no one remembers the original safety rationale.
claude::65feb150339293c4489e88821adb3d8d.what_explanation: The student provides clear, specific definitions of both workarounds and normalization of deviance, supported by concrete examples (wristband scanning, ADC overrides, Challenger disaster), fully meeting the rubric criterion for a score of 2.
claude::65feb150339293c4489e88821adb3d8d.why_evidence: I had been thinking of safety culture as a matter of attitudes and intentions. The class reframed it as a matter of small accumulated habits, most of which form below the threshold of explicit team conversation. The personal piece was recognizing that I will be present at the formation of workarounds throughout my career — and that the most useful thing I can do is name them out loud the first time, before they normalize. That requires a kind of social courage I will need to practice before I can deploy it.
claude::65feb150339293c4489e88821adb3d8d.why_explanation: The student clearly articulates why the learning mattered by describing a genuine shift in their mental model of safety culture and connecting it to a personal insight about their future role and the social courage required, fully satisfying the rubric's criteria for personal insight supported with examples.
claude::65feb150339293c4489e88821adb3d8d.how_evidence: (1) on each rotation, keeping a private list of workarounds I observe and noting which ones a senior team member would have flagged at first sight; (2) practicing the language of naming a workaround respectfully in low-stakes moments (e.g., 'I noticed we're scanning the printed wristband — is that a workflow we want?'); and (3) when I am eventually a preceptor or supervisor, treating the first-week questions a new hire asks as a free audit of my unit's workarounds
claude::65feb150339293c4489e88821adb3d8d.how_explanation: The student outlines three distinct, concrete, and actionable steps spanning immediate rotations through future supervisory roles, with specific language and behaviors described, fully meeting the rubric's standard for a score of 2.

claude::0ff36004f84f3a83bc0c71254461cf7c.what_evidence: pharmacist error rates rise measurably after 8 hours of continuous verification work and that night-shift error rates are systematically higher than day-shift even after controlling for case mix. We reviewed the alarm-fatigue evidence — typical hospital monitor produces hundreds of alarms per patient per day, the great majority of which are non-actionable, with documented patient deaths from clinicians silencing alarms that turned out to be real.
claude::0ff36004f84f3a83bc0c71254461cf7c.what_explanation: The student provides specific, detailed descriptions of key concepts including quantified claims about error rates, alarm volumes, and documented patient harms, as well as the policy responses reviewed in class — well beyond vague summary.
claude::0ff36004f84f3a83bc0c71254461cf7c.why_evidence: I had been treating my own fatigue as a willpower issue, and the class reframed it as a performance reality I should plan around (timing critical verifications earlier in shifts, taking actual breaks rather than skipping them).
claude::0ff36004f84f3a83bc0c71254461cf7c.why_explanation: The student clearly explains why the learning mattered by connecting the evidence to a personal misconception that was corrected, demonstrating genuine insight supported by a concrete example of how their thinking changed.
claude::0ff36004f84f3a83bc0c71254461cf7c.how_evidence: (1) on rotations, observing how my own attention and accuracy trend across a shift and noting when I should escalate or defer; (2) asking my preceptor whether the institution has an alarm-rationalization project and what the data shows; and (3) when I am eventually responsible for staffing or scheduling, treating fatigue engineering as a primary job, not as a wellness initiative bolted on the side.
claude::0ff36004f84f3a83bc0c71254461cf7c.how_explanation: The student outlines three distinct, specific, and actionable steps spanning immediate rotation behavior, preceptor engagement, and future professional responsibility, fully meeting the rubric's criteria for a clear and actionable future plan.

llama-3.3-70b::0ff36004f84f3a83bc0c71254.why_evidence: What this was important for was understanding that fatigue is not a personal weakness to be overcome with effort but a system property that the organization either designs around or doesn't.
llama-3.3-70b::0ff36004f84f3a83bc0c71254.why_explanation: The reflection clearly explains why the learning mattered, including the shift in perspective from viewing fatigue as a personal weakness to recognizing it as a system property, which supports a score of 2 on the WHY dimension of the rubric.

claude::76e1bd714465723ce8603c0b50d8d290.what_evidence: plain-language labeling (e.g., 'take 1 pill in the morning and 1 pill at night' rather than 'take 1 BID'), pictogram-supported instructions, teach-back at every counseling encounter, and use of the institution's professional medical interpreters rather than family members for limited-English-proficiency patients. The class also covered the AHRQ Universal Precautions toolkit, which assumes every patient may have low literacy
claude::76e1bd714465723ce8603c0b50d8d290.what_explanation: The student provides a clear and specific description of key concepts covered, including statistics, concrete examples of misinterpretation, named evidence-based tools, and specific organizational frameworks, fully meeting the rubric's criteria for a score of 2.
claude::76e1bd714465723ce8603c0b50d8d290.why_evidence: I had been thinking of counseling as the closing step of a transaction. The class made clear that for many patients, the counseling encounter is the only safety net between the prescription and a harmful self-administration error. The Universal Precautions framing also addressed something I had been quietly uncomfortable with — the temptation to assess literacy by appearance, which is both inaccurate and ethically problematic.
claude::76e1bd714465723ce8603c0b50d8d290.why_explanation: The student clearly explains why the learning mattered by articulating a personal prior misconception, how it was corrected, and connecting it to an ethical discomfort they had previously experienced, demonstrating genuine personal insight supported with specific examples.
claude::76e1bd714465723ce8603c0b50d8d290.how_evidence: (1) committing to use teach-back at every counseling encounter on my retail rotation, even when the patient seems fluent and informed; (2) using only professional interpreters when the patient is limited-English-proficient and pushing back if the workflow tries to use family; and (3) reviewing the institution's prescription label conventions and proposing plain-language improvements wherever I see jargon.
claude::76e1bd714465723ce8603c0b50d8d290.how_explanation: The student outlines three distinct, specific, and actionable steps tied to a named upcoming rotation and real workflow scenarios, fully satisfying the rubric's requirement for a clear and actionable future plan.

llama-3.3-70b::76e1bd714465723ce8603c0b5.what_evidence: Today's class focused on health literacy as a patient safety issue. I learned that approximately 36% of U.S. adults have basic or below-basic health literacy and that medication errors cluster heavily in this population — patients who cannot reliably read prescription labels, who misinterpret 'take twice daily' as 'take with two meals,' or who lack the schema to understand what a side effect is.
llama-3.3-70b::76e1bd714465723ce8603c0b5.what_explanation: The student provides a clear and specific description of key learnings, including statistics and examples of how health literacy affects patient safety, which aligns with a score of 2 on the WHAT dimension of the rubric.
llama-3.3-70b::76e1bd714465723ce8603c0b5.why_evidence: What this was important for was reframing patient counseling as a primary safety control rather than a courtesy. I had been thinking of counseling as the closing step of a transaction. The class made clear that for many patients, the counseling encounter is the only safety net between the prescription and a harmful self-administration error.

llama-3.3-70b::76e1bd714465723ce8603c0b5.how_evidence: I will apply this by (1) committing to use teach-back at every counseling encounter on my retail rotation, even when the patient seems fluent and informed; (2) using only professional interpreters when the patient is limited-English-proficient and pushing back if the workflow tries to use family; and (3) reviewing the institution's prescription label conventions and proposing plain-language improvements wherever I see jargon.

claude::aad4fbab3234afbb476f2a9d7a6b727b.what_evidence: Published rates suggest 20-30% of patients experience a medication-related adverse event within 30 days of discharge, and a meaningful fraction lead to ED visits or readmission. We reviewed the interventions with the strongest evidence: meds-to-beds programs (filling discharge prescriptions before the patient leaves), post-discharge follow-up calls within 48-72 hours, and structured medication action plans given to the patient in writing.
claude::aad4fbab3234afbb476f2a9d7a6b727b.what_explanation: The reflection provides a clear and specific description of the topic (post-discharge medication errors), includes concrete statistics, and names specific evidence-based interventions, fully meeting the rubric criteria for a score of 2.
claude::aad4fbab3234afbb476f2a9d7a6b727b.why_evidence: the discharge moment is not the end of inpatient care but the riskiest handoff in the entire hospitalization — and that pharmacy is the team that can most effectively close that gap. The personal piece was a clearer sense of what kind of pharmacy work I find most motivating; transitions-of-care work sits at the intersection of operational systems and direct patient care, and the data on its impact is strong.
claude::aad4fbab3234afbb476f2a9d7a6b727b.why_explanation: The student clearly explains why the learning matters both systemically and personally, connecting the high-risk nature of discharge handoffs to pharmacy's unique role and to their own professional motivations, satisfying the rubric's requirement for personal insight supported with examples.
claude::aad4fbab3234afbb476f2a9d7a6b727b.how_evidence: I will apply this by (1) requesting a transitions-of-care rotation with a specific focus on post-discharge follow-up calls; (2) shadowing a meds-to-beds workflow at one institution to understand its operational details; and (3) keeping a personal log during my training of the specific medication-related issues that surface in post-discharge follow-up calls — that is the data I will use when I am eventually advocating for these services.
claude::aad4fbab3234afbb476f2a9d7a6b727b.how_explanation: The student outlines three distinct, concrete, and actionable steps with clear purposes, going well beyond vague future intent and fully meeting the rubric's criteria for a score of 2.

llama-3.3-70b::aad4fbab3234afbb476f2a9d7.what_evidence: Today's session looked at post-discharge medication errors — events that happen after the patient leaves the hospital with their new medication regimen. I learned that the post-discharge period is one of the highest-risk periods in healthcare: patients are recovering, their medication list has often changed substantially during admission, the discharge counseling is frequently rushed, and the connection to the outpatient pharmacist is often absent.

The personal piece was a clearer sense of what kind of pharmacy work I find most motivating; transitions-of-care work sits at the intersection of operational systems and direct patient care, and the data on its impact is strong.

We discussed the cognitive contributors (anchoring, premature closure, availability bias) and the system contributors (test result follow-up gaps, EHR-mediated communication failures, fragmented care). The pharmacy angle was instructive: pharmacists frequently catch diagnostic errors at the point of dispensing â the reflexively prescribed antibiotic for a viral illness, the steroid without a clear indication, the duplicate therapy that suggests an unmade diagnosis.

claude::7e3cd7f15a08fb78ebf1fc0c2e4c0075.why_evidence: I had been thinking of 'safety' almost synonymously with 'medication safety.' The class reframed it as a broader field where pharmacists have particular leverage on diagnostic safety precisely because we see prescriptions across the entire care continuum. The personal piece was a heightened sense of professional latitude â when a prescription doesn't make clinical sense, asking the question is not stepping outside my role; it is fully within it.
claude::7e3cd7f15a08fb78ebf1fc0c2e4c0075.why_explanation: The student clearly explains why the learning mattered by articulating a personal conceptual shift and connecting it to a broader professional identity insight. The reflection goes beyond stating general importance and includes specific personal insight supported by reasoning about the pharmacist's unique position in the care continuum.

llama-3.3-70b::7e3cd7f15a08fb78ebf1fc0c2.what_evidence: Today's class introduced diagnostic error as one of the most under-recognized categories of patient safety harm — and one where pharmacy involvement is growing. I learned that the National Academy of Medicine's 2015 report Improving Diagnosis in Healthcare estimated that every adult will experience at least one meaningful diagnostic error in their lifetime, and that diagnostic errors contribute to about 10% of patient deaths.
llama-3.3-70b::7e3cd7f15a08fb78ebf1fc0c2.what_explanation: The student provides a clear and specific description of key learnings, including the introduction of diagnostic error and its significance, which aligns with a score of 2 on the WHAT dimension of the rubric. The evidence shows the student has a good understanding of the topic and can describe it in detail.
llama-3.3-70b::7e3cd7f15a08fb78ebf1fc0c2.why_evidence: What this was important for was widening my understanding of patient safety beyond medication errors. I had been thinking of 'safety' almost synonymously with 'medication safety.' The class reframed it as a broader field where pharmacists have particular leverage on diagnostic safety precisely because we see prescriptions across the entire care continuum.
llama-3.3-70b::7e3cd7f15a08fb78ebf1fc0c2.why_explanation: The student clearly explains why the learning mattered, including how it broadened their understanding of patient safety and reframed their perspective on the role of pharmacists, which supports a score of 2 on the WHY dimension. The evidence demonstrates personal insight and understanding of the topic's importance.
llama-3.3-70b::7e3cd7f15a08fb78ebf1fc0c2.how_evidence: I will apply this by (1) reading the National Academy of Medicine's Improving Diagnosis report executive summary before my advanced rotations; (2) practicing the specific language pharmacists use to raise diagnostic questions with prescribers respectfully; and (3) flagging for my preceptor at least one prescription per week during a clinical rotation where I think the underlying diagnosis is uncertain, even if the prescription itself is valid.
llama-3.3-70b::7e3cd7f15a08fb78ebf1fc0c2.how_explanation: The student provides a clear, specific, and actionable future plan for applying the learning, including concrete steps such as reading a report, practicing language, and flagging prescriptions, which aligns with a score of 2 on the HOW dimension. The evidence shows the student has a well-thought-out plan to integrate the learning into their practice.

claude::87c19cc109ebb185008bba98802d8b87.what_evidence: patients of color, women, and patients with non-English primary language experience measurably higher rates of medication errors, delayed diagnoses, and inadequate pain management — and that these disparities persist after adjusting for clinical and socioeconomic factors, indicating bias as a contributor. We reviewed the evidence on implicit bias training (mixed — single sessions show small short-term effects), and on structural interventions that work better: standardized order sets that limit clinician discretion at decision points where bias operates, default opt-out interpreters, and explicit equity dashboards
claude::87c19cc109ebb185008bba98802d8b87.what_explanation: The student provides a clear and specific description of key concepts covered, including named populations, specific types of disparities, evidence on training effectiveness, and concrete structural interventions. This far exceeds vague description and meets the rubric's highest standard.
claude::87c19cc109ebb185008bba98802d8b87.why_evidence: I cannot exempt myself from bias by being a well-intentioned person, and that the right response is structural rather than purely attitudinal. The personal piece was uncomfortable in productive ways — the data on disparities in pain management is severe, and as a future pharmacist I will be one of the checkpoints in that pathway.
claude::87c19cc109ebb185008bba98802d8b87.why_explanation: The student clearly explains why the learning mattered by connecting it to personal accountability and their future role as a pharmacist, and supports the analysis with specific reference to pain management disparities and the limits of individual goodwill. This demonstrates genuine personal insight backed by evidence.
claude::87c19cc109ebb185008bba98802d8b87.how_evidence: (1) reviewing the IHI Pursuing Equity initiative and the AHA's Health Equity Roadmap before my first rotation so I am fluent in the structural framing; (2) asking my hospital preceptor whether the institution stratifies any safety outcome data by demographic and, if not, naming that as a gap; and (3) committing to use teach-back and professional interpreter services with no exceptions on rotation
claude::87c19cc109ebb185008bba98802d8b87.how_explanation: The student outlines three distinct, specific, and actionable steps with named resources, a concrete timeline, and identifiable behaviors to adopt on rotation. This fully meets the rubric's criteria for a clear and actionable future plan.

llama-3.3-70b::87c19cc109ebb185008bba988.what_evidence: Today's class focused on implicit bias and equity as patient safety issues. I learned that patients of color, women, and patients with non-English primary language experience measurably higher rates of medication errors, delayed diagnoses, and inadequate pain management — and that these disparities persist after adjusting for clinical and socioeconomic factors, indicating bias as a contributor.
llama-3.3-70b::87c19cc109ebb185008bba988.what_explanation: The student provides a clear and specific description of key learnings, including the topics covered in class and the evidence related to implicit bias and equity, meeting the criteria for a score of 2 on the WHAT dimension. The description is detailed and well-defined, indicating a strong understanding of the subject matter.
llama-3.3-70b::87c19cc109ebb185008bba988.why_evidence: What this was important for was recognizing that I cannot exempt myself from bias by being a well-intentioned person, and that the right response is structural rather than purely attitudinal.
llama-3.3-70b::87c19cc109ebb185008bba988.why_explanation: The student clearly explains why the learning mattered, including personal insight into the importance of recognizing and addressing implicit bias, and provides evidence to support this understanding, meeting the criteria for a score of 2 on the WHY dimension. The student's reflection demonstrates a deep understanding of the significance of the topic.
llama-3.3-70b::87c19cc109ebb185008bba988.how_evidence: I will apply this by (1) reviewing the IHI Pursuing Equity initiative and the AHA's Health Equity Roadmap before my first rotation so I am fluent in the structural framing; (2) asking my hospital preceptor whether the institution stratifies any safety outcome data by demographic and, if not, naming that as a gap; and (3) committing to use teach-back and professional interpreter services with no exceptions on rotation, because those are the structural defaults that don't require my individual bias to be perfect.
llama-3.3-70b::87c19cc109ebb185008bba988.how_explanation: The student provides a clear, specific, and actionable future plan for applying the learning, including concrete steps to be taken on rotation, meeting the criteria for a score of 2 on the HOW dimension. llama-3.3-70b::0d96aedd6eee2875d3acbc067.what_evidence: Today's session covered telehealth and remote prescribing safety as a patient safety frontier that grew rapidly during the COVID-19 pandemic and has not retreated. I learned that the safety profile of telehealth is mixed: for chronic disease management, telehealth shows comparable safety to in-person care; for acute conditions and for new patients, the absence of physical examination and unfamiliar prescribing context creates new failure modes — including controlled-substance e-prescribing without established prescriber-patient relationships, prescription cascades through platform-based DTC (direct-to-consumer) telehealth, and inadequate medication reconciliation.
llama-3.3-70b::0d96aedd6eee2875d3acbc067.what_explanation: The student provides a clear and specific description of key learnings, activities, or concepts, meeting the criteria for a score of 2, as they detail the topics covered in the session and the mixed safety profile of telehealth. llama-3.3-70b::0d96aedd6eee2875d3acbc067.why_explanation: The student clearly explains why the learning mattered, including personal insight and supporting evidence, meeting the criteria for a score of 2, as they reflect on the importance of their role in identifying problematic prescribing patterns and the ethical implications of refusal to dispense.
llama-3.3-70b::0d96aedd6eee2875d3acbc067.how_evidence: I will apply this by (1) reading the Federation of State Medical Boards telehealth guidance and my state board's pharmacy telehealth rules; (2) during my retail rotation, asking my preceptor about the site's policy for prescriptions originating from DTC telehealth platforms and what patterns trigger refusal; and (3) committing to escalate any telehealth prescription that doesn't fit the patient's clinical history, even if it is from a recognized platform — escalation is not the same as refusal, but it documents the concern.

claude::b93386529e3c9f78ba4af26bc104d4c2.why_evidence: it shifted how I think about compounding as a career path. Before today, I thought compounding was just about making personalized medications, but now I see it as a highly regulated, patient safety-critical practice. A single failure in the sterile environment can lead to contaminated products, patient infections, or even death--events that have actually occurred due to compounding pharmacy failures. This made me appreciate why sterile compounders must be extremely meticulous. I also realized that my initial assumption--that compounding was a relatively unregulated niche area of pharmacy--was completely wrong.

claude::59d7d891867df9c5cf27ff1070643b9d.what_evidence: USP <797> establishes requirements for personnel training and competency assessment--including gloved fingertip sampling, media fill tests, and garbing competency evaluations--as well as environmental monitoring and beyond-use dating (BUDs) for sterile preparations based on compounding category and risk level. USP <800> addresses the safe handling of hazardous drugs as defined by the NIOSH Hazardous Drug List, requiring containment primary engineering controls (C-PECs), appropriate personal protective equipment, and defined procedures for spill management and waste disposal to protect healthcare workers from drug exposure. I also learned that ISO classifications define air quality in cleanroom environments: ISO 5 (critical zone), ISO 7 (buffer area), and ISO 8 (ante-area) each carry specific requirements for particulate counts and pressure differentials to maintain sterility.
claude::59d7d891867df9c5cf27ff1070643b9d.what_explanation: The student provides a clear and specific description of multiple key concepts including USP <797>, USP <800>, and ISO classifications, naming concrete elements such as gloved fingertip sampling, C-PECs, and pressure differentials. This meets the rubric's highest standard for detailed, specific description of learning.
claude::59d7d891867df9c5cf27ff1070643b9d.why_evidence: Before today, I understood that compounding had rules, but I had not appreciated how rigorously evidence-based and consequential those rules are. Contamination of sterile products has caused real patient harm--including the 2012 fungal meningitis outbreak linked to a compounding pharmacy in Massachusetts that resulted in over 750 illnesses and 64 deaths. Understanding that BUDs are assigned based on facility conditions and sterility testing--not arbitrary timelines--shifted how I think about precision in compounding practice.
claude::59d7d891867df9c5cf27ff1070643b9d.why_explanation: The student clearly explains why the learning mattered by contrasting prior understanding with new insight, supporting the analysis with a specific real-world example (the 2012 meningitis outbreak) and articulating a concrete conceptual shift regarding BUD assignment. This satisfies the rubric's criteria for personal insight supported by evidence.
claude::59d7d891867df9c5cf27ff1070643b9d.how_evidence: Going forward, I will apply this learning at my internship by: (1) reviewing the site's standard operating procedures (SOPs) for sterile compounding and comparing them against current USP <797> and <800> requirements; (2) volunteering to observe environmental monitoring activities and competency assessments such as gloved fingertip sampling; and (3) completing all required garbing and media fill training before working independently in any cleanroom environment.
claude::59d7d891867df9c5cf27ff1070643b9d.how_explanation: The student outlines three distinct, actionable steps tied to a specific setting (their internship), referencing concrete activities like SOP review, observation of environmental monitoring, and completion of garbing and media fill training. This clearly meets the rubric's highest standard for a specific and actionable future plan.

claude::a36516f4ad68bea8c96518572accfda2.what_evidence: drugs are classified into five federal schedules (Schedules I-V) based on medical utility and potential for abuse or physical or psychological dependence. Schedule I substances (e.g., heroin, LSD) have no accepted medical use and cannot be prescribed, while Schedules II-V are available by prescription with increasingly flexible requirements. Schedule II medications (e.g., oxycodone, fentanyl, amphetamines) require a new prescription each time--no refills are permitted--and must be ordered from suppliers using DEA Form 222 or the equivalent Controlled Substance Ordering System (CSOS). Schedules III-V allow up to five refills within six months.
claude::a36516f4ad68bea8c96518572accfda2.what_explanation: The student provides a clear and specific description of key learnings, naming specific schedules, example drugs, refill rules, ordering systems, inventory requirements, and disposal pathways. This far exceeds vague description and meets the rubric's highest standard.
claude::a36516f4ad68bea8c96518572accfda2.why_evidence: I had previously thought of controlled substance management primarily as administrative overhead--lots of forms and counts--but today's class reframed it as a critical public health and patient safety function. Given the ongoing opioid epidemic, I now understand more concretely how pharmacists serve as a key checkpoint in preventing diversion while still ensuring that patients with genuine clinical needs can access these medications.
claude::a36516f4ad68bea8c96518572accfda2.why_explanation: The student clearly explains why the learning mattered by articulating a shift in personal perspective and connecting it to a real-world public health context (the opioid epidemic), demonstrating genuine personal insight supported with concrete reasoning. This satisfies the rubric's highest standard for analysis.
claude::a36516f4ad68bea8c96518572accfda2.how_evidence: (1) during my inpatient internship, review the facility's controlled substance policies and ask to shadow or assist with biennial inventory and daily count discrepancy resolution; (2) access my state's PMP system and practice running a patient's controlled substance history before filling a prescription to understand what patterns might trigger further review; and (3) ask my preceptor to walk me through the documentation and reporting process for a controlled substance discrepancy before I encounter one independently.
claude::a36516f4ad68bea8c96518572accfda2.how_explanation: The student outlines three specific, actionable steps tied to real upcoming opportunities (internship, PMP access, preceptor guidance), each with a clear purpose, fully meeting the rubric's criteria for a clear, specific, and actionable future plan.

claude::37ecbfbf70e00171158d483568e1a1cf.what_evidence: HIPAA--the Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act--is composed of two key rules especially relevant to pharmacy practice: the Privacy Rule, which governs the use and disclosure of Protected Health Information (PHI), and the Security Rule, which applies specifically to electronic PHI (ePHI). PHI is defined as individually identifiable health information linked to any of 18 specific identifiers--such as name, address, dates of service, prescription numbers, or biometric data--that relates to a patient's health status, care, or payment for care.
claude::37ecbfbf70e00171158d483568e1a1cf.what_explanation: The student provides a clear and specific description of key HIPAA concepts including both rules, the definition of PHI with concrete examples of identifiers, the minimum necessary standard, TPO disclosures, and tiered penalty structures. This level of detail and specificity meets the Score 2 criteria.
claude::37ecbfbf70e00171158d483568e1a1cf.why_evidence: This is important for my development as a pharmacist because patients must trust that their health information is handled with care. Without that trust, patients may withhold information that is critical to their safe care--putting themselves at risk.
claude::37ecbfbf70e00171158d483568e1a1cf.why_explanation: The student states a general reason why HIPAA matters and offers a brief logical consequence (patients withholding information), but the insight remains surface-level and lacks a personal example or deeper reflection on how this learning specifically affected the student's thinking or practice. This aligns with Score 1.
claude::37ecbfbf70e00171158d483568e1a1cf.how_evidence: I will make sure to follow HIPAA standards when I work in pharmacy settings and be careful about how I share patient information.
claude::37ecbfbf70e00171158d483568e1a1cf.how_explanation: The student expresses a future intent to follow HIPAA standards, but the plan is vague and not actionable—it does not specify concrete behaviors, situations, or strategies for applying the learning. This aligns with Score 1.

claude::f5a5f601748c7ca8bf435ab77b3b6a2d.what_evidence: The Privacy Rule protects Protected Health Information (PHI), defined as individually identifiable information linked to any of 18 specific identifiers (such as name, date of birth, address, or prescription number) that relates to a patient's health, treatment, or payment for care. The Security Rule extends these protections to electronic PHI (ePHI) and requires covered entities to implement administrative, physical, and technical safeguards.
claude::f5a5f601748c7ca8bf435ab77b3b6a2d.what_explanation: The student provides a clear and specific description of multiple HIPAA concepts including PHI, ePHI, the minimum necessary standard, BAAs, and breach notification rules with precise details such as the 18 identifiers, TPO exceptions, and the 60-day notification window. This fully meets the rubric criterion for a score of 2.
claude::f5a5f601748c7ca8bf435ab77b3b6a2d.why_evidence: early in my pharmacy experience, I observed a colleague reading out a patient's medication list at the front counter while other customers were nearby. At the time I felt uncomfortable but was unsure whether it was a HIPAA violation or simply poor practice. Today's class clarified that verbal disclosures of PHI in non-private settings where unauthorized individuals could overhear do constitute HIPAA violations. This reframed my understanding: HIPAA compliance is not primarily about securing computers or signing forms--it is an active behavioral standard embedded in every counter interaction, phone call, and counseling session.
claude::f5a5f601748c7ca8bf435ab77b3b6a2d.why_explanation: The student goes beyond stating general importance by connecting the learning to a specific personal experience, explaining how the session resolved prior uncertainty and fundamentally reframed their conceptual understanding of HIPAA compliance. This demonstrates clear personal insight supported by a concrete example, satisfying the rubric criteria for a score of 2.
claude::f5a5f601748c7ca8bf435ab77b3b6a2d.how_evidence: (1) consistently moving to a private counseling area whenever discussing sensitive medication information with a patient, even under time pressure; (2) asking my preceptor at my internship to walk me through the site's breach investigation and notification protocol so I know what to do if I witness or accidentally cause a disclosure; and (3) reviewing the site's Business Associate Agreements to understand which vendors have access to patient data and for what purposes.
claude::f5a5f601748c7ca8bf435ab77b3b6a2d.how_explanation: The student outlines three distinct, specific, and immediately actionable steps tied directly to the content learned, including behavioral changes, preceptor engagement, and document review. This exceeds a vague future intent and fully meets the rubric criterion for a score of 2.

claude::7bffe9e7e655334baeb6ce1f5e94be8c.what_evidence: Key elements include the requirement for trading partners--manufacturers, wholesale distributors, repackagers, and dispensers including pharmacies--to exchange three specific transaction documents: Transaction Information (TI), Transaction History (TH), and Transaction Statement (TS), collectively referred to as T3 documentation. I also learned about drug product serialization: every prescription drug package must carry a standardized Drug Product Identifier (DPI) encoded in a 2D data matrix barcode containing the National Drug Code (NDC), lot number, expiration date, and a unique serial number.
claude::7bffe9e7e655334baeb6ce1f5e94be8c.what_explanation: The student provides a clear and specific description of the DSCSA's key components, naming exact document types, stakeholders, serialization requirements, and regulatory milestones. This level of detail fully meets the rubric's criteria for a Score 2.
claude::7bffe9e7e655334baeb6ce1f5e94be8c.why_evidence: I had not previously thought of a drug's arrival at the pharmacy shelf as the endpoint of a regulated, documented chain of custody. Nor had I considered that pharmacists are active participants in supply chain security rather than just clinical care providers. Drug counterfeiting, diversion, and contamination are real threats that can directly harm patients--and the DSCSA exists to close the gaps where these threats can enter the supply chain.
claude::7bffe9e7e655334baeb6ce1f5e94be8c.why_explanation: The student articulates a genuine shift in personal understanding, explains why the learning matters with concrete examples of real-world threats, and connects the legislation directly to expanded pharmacist responsibility. This demonstrates personal insight supported by evidence, satisfying the Score 2 criteria.
claude::7bffe9e7e655334baeb6ce1f5e94be8c.how_evidence: (1) asking my preceptor at my internship to explain the site's process for receiving pharmaceutical shipments and how T3 documentation is reviewed and stored; (2) reviewing the FDA's published guidance on suspect and illegitimate product investigation procedures so I understand the procedural steps before I encounter one; and (3) asking the pharmacy team whether their dispensing system supports 2D barcode scanning for DSCSA verification and what the manual fallback process is when the system is unavailable.
claude::7bffe9e7e655334baeb6ce1f5e94be8c.how_explanation: The student outlines three distinct, specific, and immediately actionable steps tied directly to the content learned, including named resources and concrete questions to ask. This clearly meets the Score 2 rubric criteria for a specific and actionable future plan.

claude::c3dadf0330001f8f3e71c2dc97797630.what_evidence: Each state's Board of Pharmacy promulgates regulations on scope of practice, technician-to-pharmacist ratios, pharmacist-in-charge responsibilities, continuing education requirements (typically 15–30 CE hours per year, with required hours in patient safety, opioid management, and immunization), and disciplinary processes for license violations. We also reviewed the Multistate Pharmacy Licensure Compact and the difference between license reciprocity by score transfer (NAPLEX score transfer) and reciprocity by examination
claude::c3dadf0330001f8f3e71c2dc97797630.what_explanation: The student provides a clear and specific description of key concepts covered, including concrete details such as CE hour ranges, required topic areas, and specific regulatory distinctions like the Multistate Pharmacy Licensure Compact versus reciprocity types, fully meeting the Score 2 criteria.
claude::c3dadf0330001f8f3e71c2dc97797630.why_evidence: I had been thinking of 'pharmacy regulation' mostly in federal terms — DEA, FDA, HIPAA — and had not appreciated that the state board has the most direct day-to-day authority over how I practice and whether I keep my license. Realizing that the rules for things like remote verification, technician scope, and CE compliance vary by state changed how I think about taking a job out of state
claude::c3dadf0330001f8f3e71c2dc97797630.why_explanation: The student clearly explains why the learning mattered by articulating a prior misconception, demonstrating genuine personal insight about the shift in their thinking, and supporting it with specific examples such as remote verification and out-of-state job considerations, fully meeting the Score 2 criteria.
claude::c3dadf0330001f8f3e71c2dc97797630.how_evidence: (1) downloading and reading my state's Pharmacy Practice Act and accompanying regulations before I graduate, with particular attention to the technician scope, remote verification rules, and CE requirements; (2) signing up for my state board's email list so I receive regulatory updates and disciplinary actions, which I will read for the lessons-learned content; and (3) building a personal CE-tracking spreadsheet from year one of practice so I never end a renewal cycle short on hours or topic-required content.
claude::c3dadf0330001f8f3e71c2dc97797630.how_explanation: The student outlines three distinct, specific, and actionable steps with clear timelines and purposes, going well beyond vague future intent and fully satisfying the Score 2 criteria for integration.

claude::fa10abdbc798570ba24af6fdd7c0de76.what_evidence: REMS elements can include Medication Guides, Communication Plans, and Elements to Assure Safe Use (ETASU) such as required prescriber certification, mandatory patient enrollment, lab monitoring (e.g., absolute neutrophil count for clozapine), and certified-pharmacy registration. We also reviewed the move toward shared system REMS (e.g., the Transmucosal Immediate-Release Fentanyl REMS, the iPLEDGE REMS for isotretinoin) where multiple manufacturers participate in a unified program.
claude::fa10abdbc798570ba24af6fdd7c0de76.what_explanation: The reflection provides clear, specific descriptions of REMS components, named examples (clozapine, iPLEDGE, TIRF), and concrete regulatory details, fully meeting the rubric's standard for a Score 2.
claude::fa10abdbc798570ba24af6fdd7c0de76.why_evidence: This shifted my picture of post-market drug regulation. I had thought of FDA approval as a single binary event. The REMS framework reframes it as a conditional approval where the manufacturer accepts ongoing risk-management obligations, and the pharmacist becomes one of the points where those obligations are operationalized. Personally, this clarified that REMS errors are not minor administrative slips — failing to verify clozapine ANC or filling an isotretinoin prescription without a current iPLEDGE confirmation is both unsafe for the patient and a regulatory violation for the pharmacy.
claude::fa10abdbc798570ba24af6fdd7c0de76.why_explanation: The student articulates a meaningful conceptual shift (binary vs. conditional approval), offers personal insight about the pharmacist's role, and supports the analysis with specific examples of consequences, satisfying all criteria for a Score 2.
claude::fa10abdbc798570ba24af6fdd7c0de76.how_evidence: I will apply this by (1) asking my retail and inpatient preceptors to walk me through how their site handles three current REMS programs (likely clozapine, isotretinoin, and TIRF) so I see the actual workflow rather than the abstract rule; (2) maintaining a personal reference list of the active REMS programs relevant to my practice setting; and (3) committing, when I encounter a REMS-controlled prescription, to verify the REMS element rather than assume the prescriber's office has already done so — the pharmacy is a co-equal responsible party.
claude::fa10abdbc798570ba24af6fdd7c0de76.how_explanation: The plan is specific, numbered, and immediately actionable, naming particular programs, concrete behaviors, and a clear rationale for each step, fully meeting the rubric's Score 2 criteria.

llama-3.3-70b::fa10abdbc798570ba24af6fdd.why_explanation: The reflection clearly explains why the learning mattered, including personal insight into how the understanding of REMS programs reframed the student's perspective on post-market drug regulation, which supports a score of 2.
llama-3.3-70b::fa10abdbc798570ba24af6fdd.how_evidence: I will apply this by (1) asking my retail and inpatient preceptors to walk me through how their site handles three current REMS programs (likely clozapine, isotretinoin, and TIRF) so I see the actual workflow rather than the abstract rule; (2) maintaining a personal reference list of the active REMS programs relevant to my practice setting; and (3) committing, when I encounter a REMS-controlled prescription, to verify the REMS element rather than assume the prescriber's office has already done so — the pharmacy is a co-equal responsible party.

claude::dcb4c70685caab3cd01ecc48da865eb4.what_evidence: 503A pharmacies compound on receipt of a prescription for an identified patient, are regulated primarily by state boards under USP <797> and <800>, and may not produce drugs that are essentially copies of commercially available products. 503B outsourcing facilities, created by the Drug Quality and Security Act after the 2012 NECC fungal meningitis outbreak, register with the FDA, follow current Good Manufacturing Practice (cGMP), and may produce sterile compounds in bulk for office stock without patient-specific prescriptions, subject to FDA inspection.
claude::dcb4c70685caab3cd01ecc48da865eb4.what_explanation: The student provides a clear and specific description of both 503A and 503B frameworks, including regulatory bodies, standards, and operational distinctions. This meets the rubric's highest standard for detailed, specific description of key learnings.
claude::dcb4c70685caab3cd01ecc48da865eb4.why_evidence: What this learning crystallized for me was why the 503A/503B framework exists at all — it is the regulatory response to a real, recent harm event (NECC) that killed 64 people. The distinction is not bureaucratic; it is the way the system tries to ensure that bulk sterile compounding carries manufacturer-grade oversight while preserving the ability of community and hospital pharmacies to compound for specific patients. I had previously thought of compounding regulation as monolithic; understanding the two-tier structure clarified the trade-offs that hospital pharmacy directors actually make.
claude::dcb4c70685caab3cd01ecc48da865eb4.why_explanation: The student clearly explains why the learning mattered by connecting it to a real harm event, articulating the policy rationale, and sharing a personal insight about how their prior understanding was corrected. This demonstrates genuine analysis supported with specific examples, meeting the rubric's highest standard.
claude::dcb4c70685caab3cd01ecc48da865eb4.how_evidence: I will apply this by (1) asking my hospital preceptor whether the site uses 503B outsourcing for any sterile compounded products and, if so, how the supplier was vetted; (2) reading at least one FDA inspection report (Form 483) of a 503B facility to understand what cGMP findings actually look like; and (3) being able, by the end of my advanced rotations, to articulate coherently when 503A versus 503B sourcing is appropriate so I can contribute to that conversation as a new pharmacist.
claude::dcb4c70685caab3cd01ecc48da865eb4.how_explanation: The student outlines three distinct, specific, and actionable steps with clear timelines and concrete activities, fully satisfying the rubric's criteria for a clear and actionable future plan.

claude::2c074fe2e643dfdbd045cb7d91431da9.what_evidence: I learned the structure of the IND/NDA process — preclinical, Phase I (safety, small healthy-volunteer), Phase II (efficacy, dose-finding), Phase III (large randomized comparison), NDA submission and FDA review — and the difference between standard, priority, breakthrough, and accelerated approval designations. We also reviewed Phase IV / post-market surveillance: REMS, FDA Adverse Event Reporting System (FAERS), MedWatch reports, manufacturer Periodic Safety Update Reports, and label updates triggered by real-world signals.
claude::2c074fe2e643dfdbd045cb7d91431da9.what_explanation: The student provides a clear and specific enumeration of key concepts covered, including named phases, approval designations, and post-market surveillance mechanisms, fully meeting the rubric's criteria for a Score 2.
claude::2c074fe2e643dfdbd045cb7d91431da9.why_evidence: Today's class made clear that the pre-approval trials, while necessary, cannot detect rare adverse events, long-latency harms, or interactions that surface only at population scale. Pharmacovigilance is the actual safety net, and pharmacists submit a substantial portion of all MedWatch reports — meaning my future MedWatch submissions are not optional good citizenship but a structural part of how the FDA learns about drugs after approval.
claude::2c074fe2e643dfdbd045cb7d91431da9.why_explanation: The student articulates a genuine shift in prior understanding (approval as endpoint vs. ongoing surveillance), explains why the learning matters with personal insight, and supports the analysis with specific reasoning about population-scale detection and the pharmacist's structural role, satisfying all Score 2 criteria.
claude::2c074fe2e643dfdbd045cb7d91431da9.how_evidence: (1) reading the FDA MedWatch reporting form before my first rotation so the form is not new to me when I encounter a reportable event; (2) asking each preceptor about the institution's process for routing suspected ADRs and committing to file at least one MedWatch report during my rotations under preceptor supervision; and (3) building a habit, when I see a new drug being prescribed, of looking up its approval pathway and any post-market label updates
claude::2c074fe2e643dfdbd045cb7d91431da9.how_explanation: The student outlines three distinct, concrete, and actionable steps with specific timing and measurable commitments, clearly meeting the rubric's Score 2 standard for a clear and actionable future plan.

llama-3.3-70b::2c074fe2e643dfdbd045cb7d9.what_explanation: The student provides a clear and specific description of key learnings, including the FDA drug approval pathway and post-market pharmacovigilance, which aligns with a score of 2 on the WHAT dimension. claude::ac5c604a3fbbfa4674d302b57888a866.what_evidence: 340B was created in 1992 to stretch limited federal resources by enabling covered entities to use the savings to expand services to underserved patients. The compliance framework is exacting: covered entities must maintain auditable records that 340B-priced drugs went only to eligible patients, must avoid duplicate discounts (drugs purchased at 340B prices cannot also be billed at Medicaid rebate rates), and must accept HRSA audits.
claude::ac5c604a3fbbfa4674d302b57888a866.what_explanation: The student provides a clear and specific description of the 340B program's origins, eligible entities, and compliance requirements, including concrete details like duplicate discount prohibition and HRSA audits, meeting the rubric's standard for a Score 2.
claude::ac5c604a3fbbfa4674d302b57888a866.why_evidence: The personal piece was recognizing that if I work at a 340B covered entity early in my career, the compliance obligations are substantial enough that I should learn the program in detail rather than treat it as something the management team handles.
claude::ac5c604a3fbbfa4674d302b57888a866.why_explanation: The student goes beyond stating general importance by offering personal insight about their own career trajectory and a specific realization about pharmacist responsibility in compliance, supported by the earlier discussion of contract pharmacy arrangements and patient-eligibility determinations, satisfying the Score 2 criteria.
claude::ac5c604a3fbbfa4674d302b57888a866.how_evidence: (1) reviewing the HRSA 340B program manual before any rotation at a 340B-eligible site; (2) asking my preceptor to walk me through the patient-eligibility determination workflow at the site, including how the pharmacist contributes; and (3) reading at least two recent HRSA 340B audit findings to understand what compliance failures actually look like, so I am prepared to recognize them.
claude::ac5c604a3fbbfa4674d302b57888a866.how_explanation: The student outlines three distinct, specific, and actionable steps with clear triggers and purposes, fully meeting the Score 2 rubric standard for a clear and actionable future plan.

claude::19a9531258b7785b9866d3824df945cb.what_evidence: telepharmacy is primarily state-regulated, with significant variation: some states explicitly authorize telepharmacy with specific dispensing-cabinet arrangements (North Dakota was an early adopter), while others restrict it to remote verification only. The common regulatory elements are pharmacist licensure in the state of patient location, technician supervision rules that often include video monitoring requirements, and dispensing-equipment specifications.
claude::19a9531258b7785b9866d3824df945cb.what_explanation: The reflection provides clear, specific descriptions of key concepts including state regulatory variation, named examples (North Dakota), and concrete regulatory elements such as licensure requirements and dispensing-equipment specifications, fully meeting the Score 2 criteria.
claude::19a9531258b7785b9866d3824df945cb.why_evidence: I had been thinking of remote work in pharmacy as a workflow choice; the class reframed it as a regulated practice modality whose rules I need to track in any state I practice. The personal piece was recognizing that career flexibility increasingly depends on fluency with telepharmacy regulations and the tools that operationalize them.
claude::19a9531258b7785b9866d3824df945cb.why_explanation: The student clearly explains why the learning mattered by articulating a personal conceptual shift (from workflow choice to regulated modality) and connecting it to career implications, demonstrating genuine personal insight supported by specific reasoning.
claude::19a9531258b7785b9866d3824df945cb.how_evidence: (1) reading my state board's telepharmacy regulations before graduation, with attention to remote verification rules; (2) asking each retail and hospital preceptor whether their site uses any remote verification or telepharmacy and how the workflow is regulated; and (3) following the National Association of Boards of Pharmacy telepharmacy white papers so I am tracking the field as it evolves rather than relearning it later.
claude::19a9531258b7785b9866d3824df945cb.how_explanation: The student outlines three distinct, concrete, and actionable steps with specific targets (state board regulations, preceptor conversations, NABP white papers), fully satisfying the Score 2 criteria for a clear and actionable future plan.

claude::35e7260eb48f0b943426bf6232840d4f.what_evidence: OSHA's Hazard Communication Standard requires Safety Data Sheets (SDS) for all hazardous chemicals in the workplace, including the cleaning agents and reagents used in compounding. The Bloodborne Pathogens Standard applies to pharmacy staff who administer immunizations and requires specific exposure control plans, post-exposure protocols, and Hepatitis B vaccination availability. The hazardous drug handling requirements intersect with USP <800> but extend further into engineering controls (C-PECs), administrative controls (training, medical surveillance for exposed employees), and PPE specifications.
claude::35e7260eb48f0b943426bf6232840d4f.what_explanation: The student provides clear, specific descriptions of multiple distinct OSHA standards with concrete details (SDS, C-PECs, exposure control plans, Hepatitis B vaccination), fully meeting the rubric criterion for a score of 2.
claude::35e7260eb48f0b943426bf6232840d4f.why_evidence: recognizing that pharmacy regulation extends beyond drug-product rules into workplace safety in ways I had not fully appreciated. The personal piece was a clearer sense that, when I am eventually a pharmacy manager or PIC, OSHA compliance is part of the job rather than an HR concern that someone else handles.

claude::6dd333ca5667d9625e8e0e381780c12c.what_evidence: AKS prohibits the exchange of anything of value to induce or reward referrals for federally reimbursable services; that Stark prohibits physicians from referring Medicare or Medicaid patients for designated health services to entities the physician (or immediate family) has a financial relationship with; and that the False Claims Act creates liability for submitting or causing the submission of false or fraudulent claims to federal health programs.
claude::6dd333ca5667d9625e8e0e381780c12c.what_explanation: The student provides clear, specific descriptions of three distinct legal frameworks with precise definitions and pharmacy-specific examples for each, fully meeting the rubric's standard for a score of 2.
claude::6dd333ca5667d9625e8e0e381780c12c.why_evidence: I had been thinking of fraud-and-abuse compliance as something for billing departments to manage. The class made clear that pharmacist judgment about acceptable promotions, vendor relationships, and billing accuracy is on the regulatory hook personally, not just institutionally. The personal piece was understanding that the line between competitive practice and AKS violation is more specific than I had assumed and worth knowing in detail.
claude::6dd333ca5667d9625e8e0e381780c12c.why_explanation: The student articulates a meaningful shift in personal understanding — moving from viewing compliance as an institutional/billing concern to recognizing personal professional liability — supported by specific prior assumptions and concrete examples, satisfying the rubric criteria for a score of 2.
claude::6dd333ca5667d9625e8e0e381780c12c.how_evidence: (1) reading the OIG Compliance Program Guidance for Pharmacies in full before my retail management rotation; (2) when I encounter a promotion or marketing offer at a pharmacy site, asking myself whether it could be characterized as inducement and asking my preceptor; and (3) completing the institution's annual fraud-waste-abuse training rather than skimming it.
claude::6dd333ca5667d9625e8e0e381780c12c.how_explanation: The student outlines three distinct, actionable steps with specific resources, timing, and behavioral triggers, meeting the rubric's highest standard for a clear and actionable future application plan.

claude::303ee1b440569eaeecec69cf6ff96795.what_evidence: recalls are classified by severity: Class I (reasonable probability of serious adverse health consequences or death), Class II (temporary or medically reversible adverse health consequences, or remote probability of serious harm), and Class III (unlikely to cause adverse health consequences but in violation of FDA regulations). The pharmacy's responsibility on receipt of a recall notice includes immediate quarantine of affected lots, identification of patients who received the drug from current dispensing records, prescriber notification, and patient communication for Class I recalls.
claude::303ee1b440569eaeecec69cf6ff96795.what_explanation: The reflection provides clear, specific descriptions of recall classifications with precise definitions and enumerates concrete pharmacy responsibilities. It also references real-world examples (valsartan/losartan nitrosamine recalls and ranitidine), fully meeting the rubric's standard for specificity.
claude::303ee1b440569eaeecec69cf6ff96795.why_evidence: The personal piece was recognizing that recall response is a high-stakes communication exercise — a Class I recall reaches patients who are taking the medication for a real condition, and the pharmacist's clarity about what to do with their current supply, what to ask the prescriber, and how to handle the timing of switching agents has real clinical impact.
claude::303ee1b440569eaeecec69cf6ff96795.why_explanation: The student moves beyond stating general importance to articulating a personal insight about the pharmacist's direct communicative and clinical role, supported by specific examples of what that communication entails. This demonstrates genuine analytical reflection rather than a restatement of expectations.
claude::303ee1b440569eaeecec69cf6ff96795.how_evidence: (1) reviewing the FDA's recall classification page and the institution's recall SOP before my retail rotation; (2) asking my preceptor whether I can sit in on one recall communication to a patient if one occurs during the rotation; and (3) building a personal mental script for Class I recall counseling — explaining the issue clearly without alarming language, the next steps, and the prescriber's role — so the script exists before I need it.
claude::303ee1b440569eaeecec69cf6ff96795.how_explanation: The student outlines three distinct, actionable steps with specific resources, timing, and goals, fully satisfying the rubric's requirement for a clear and actionable future plan rather than vague future intent.

llama-3.3-70b::303ee1b440569eaeecec69cf6.what_explanation: The reflection provides a clear and specific description of key learnings, including the FDA's process for removing products from the market and the classification of recalls by severity, meeting the criteria for a score of 2.

The personal piece was recognizing that recall response is a high-stakes communication exercise — a Class I recall reaches patients who are taking the medication for a real condition, and the pharmacist's clarity about what to do with their current supply, what to ask the prescriber, and how to handle the timing of switching agents has real clinical impact.
llama-3.3-70b::303ee1b440569eaeecec69cf6.why_explanation: The reflection clearly explains why the learning mattered, including personal insight into the pharmacist's role in recall response and its clinical impact, supporting a score of 2.

claude::7b53925331835e13b98278135f9dceea.what_evidence: I learned that the DEA regulates the destruction of controlled substances by registrants and that improper disposal — flushing, incinerating without authorization, returning to the wholesaler without the right paperwork — creates serious compliance risk. Form 41 documents the surrender of controlled substances to a DEA-authorized destruction agent, and must be witnessed and signed by two authorized agents. We also covered the Drug Enforcement Administration's Take-Back Program and the framework for community pharmacy drug-collection receptacles, which became substantially more permissive after the 2014 final rule. The class also addressed DEA Form 222 (now the equivalent CSOS electronic ordering system) and the DEA Form 106 for reporting theft or significant loss.
claude::7b53925331835e13b98278135f9dceea.what_explanation: The student provides clear, specific descriptions of multiple distinct concepts including DEA Form 41, Form 222/CSOS, Form 106, the Take-Back Program, and the 2014 final rule, with concrete procedural details such as witness requirements and compliance risks. This meets the rubric's standard for a clear and specific description of key learnings.
claude::7b53925331835e13b98278135f9dceea.why_evidence: understanding that controlled substance documentation is a continuous obligation rather than an event-driven one. Every disposal, every loss, every theft requires specific paperwork executed within specific time windows. The personal piece was a clearer sense of the daily operational reality of controlled-substance compliance and how much of it is the PIC's personal responsibility.
claude::7b53925331835e13b98278135f9dceea.why_explanation: The student articulates a meaningful personal insight — reframing compliance as a continuous daily obligation rather than episodic — and connects it to the real-world operational responsibility of the PIC role, satisfying the rubric's requirement for personal insight supported with examples.
claude::7b53925331835e13b98278135f9dceea.how_evidence: I will apply this by (1) reviewing DEA Form 41, Form 222, and Form 106 before my retail rotation so the documents are not new to me when I encounter them; (2) asking my preceptor to walk me through the site's controlled-substance disposal log and one prior disposal event; and (3) when I am eventually in a PIC-track role, treating controlled-substance documentation as a daily discipline rather than a quarterly review item — the gaps that get found in DEA inspections almost always trace back to missed documentation that was small in any single instance.
claude::7b53925331835e13b98278135f9dceea.how_explanation: The student outlines three distinct, actionable steps with specific timelines and contexts (pre-rotation review, a concrete preceptor ask, and a long-term professional habit), fully meeting the rubric's standard for a clear, specific, and actionable future plan.
